content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Films for single ladies that aren't male-centric

**Score:** 111
**Body:** As a 31 F all the movies of single ladies "discovering themselves" end with them meeting a man. 

No thanks. 

Want a film with a badass lady THRIVING. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Gravity

Arrival

Fried Green Tomatoes

Aliens

Annihilation

Silence of the Lambs

Terminator 2
  - I would like to add Alien to the list 👽
OG Terminator is my favorite movie of all time, doesn't exactly fit criteria but worth it imo, especially if you want to watch T2
2. Muriel's Wedding
3. Mona Lisa Smile
4. Not sure if I could say the end is "Thriving", but Thelma and Louise has all sorts of discovering and is more about losing men.  
Also maybe "First Wives Club"
5. It’s older, but Norma Rae has a badass single lady and while she does have a romantic interest, it’s not the point of the film, and does not play a part in the resolution.

Winter’s Bone has a kickass main character in Jennifer Lawrence and no love interest. 

Thoroughbreds is a great candy-colored noir, they’re a lot more focused on murdering the stepfather than finding a boyfriend.

Gravity with Sandra Bullock is man-free except for the ghost of George Clooney oddly, but she does not end up with him. I thought she was pretty badass.

6. One movie I REALLY liked and I believe also very much misunderstood, and fits your criteria, is in fact, Alex Garland's Civil War (2024). The protagonist is a young photojournalist tries to find her place in the world.. nothing about relationships. It's about her experience as a person, a photographer, and most of all, a human being. Highly recommended if you haven't seen it.
7. They aren't *all* single but Oceans 8 was great 😁
8. Mrs. Harris Goes to Paris

A lady whose charming story of self-discovery has nothing to do with men. 


9. Booksmart, they aren't really badasses but it was entertaining 

## 2. Looking for films featuring unattractive, complex female protagonists, exploring themes such as depression, madness, and insanity without any romantic elements. With philosophical discussions or unique visual aesthetic would be a plus

**Score:** 54
**Body:** As title. 

I've been looking for films dealing with themes like depression, madness or insanity featuring female characters for a long time, and have visited some of them. While there are already obvious choices that have been frequently discussed, such as Girl, Interrupted or Melancholia, for me, the female characters in these films are still often too young or conventionally attractive (though I do enjoy these two I mentioned above and quite related to the characters especially when I was younger). I’d like to see older women with diverse bodies. It would be nice if the characters are not white, but it's just a plus, not necessarily a requirement. 

I'm open to experimental films, so no matter how unconventional the style, I think I can can handle it. For example, I consider Derek Jarman's Blue is a beautiful work, though in entire film there's no images, only blue color. So I'm open to all choices. Of course, I still hope the movies you recommend have images lol, I was just using that as an example to show that I’m open to less conventional narrative styles.

Thanks!! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Whatever happened to Baby Jane 
2. Not a movie but I highly HIGHLY recommend the TV show "Undone" (created by Kate Purdy and Raphael Bob-Waksberg).   
  
Philosophical, psychological, visually stunning (rotoscope animation), surreal, about mental illness and time travel. Each episode is about half an hour, there are 2 seasons with 8 episodes each so you can get through it in a weekend. The protagonist is a biracial woman who uses hearing aids and her mixed cultural heritage ties in closely with the themes and plot. While she is conventional in appearance and in her 20s, there's so much complexity to her and the story that her looks are youth are hardly the focus. There's also an intergenerational aspect, so women of different ages/eras feature.
3. Perhaps... Misery?
4. Monster? I haven’t seen it myself but it may fit your description
5. Three Billboards Outside Ebbing Missouri 
6. If you want I can just come over 
7. The Piano;
Grey Gardens
8. Can You Ever Forgive Me? (2018)
9. "Persona" 1966 and "Repulsion" respectively  almost fit what you're looking for, but I think both check enough boxes that you should definitely give them a watch.
10. If I had legs, I’d kick you 

## 3. Please recommend "one man army" movies

**Score:** 37
**Body:** Hi,

looking for movies essentially similar to "John Wick", "The Gray Man", "The extraction", "The Equalizer". 

Preferred criteria would be that action scenes wouldn't have 10000 cuts, like in "Taken" for example. The ones I listed have that "one long action sequence" vibes which is what Im looking for.

  
Thanks ! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Commando
  - First Blood
2. Sisu - 1 & 2 
3. Nobody

The Mechanic 

Any Transporter movies 
4. Die Hard
5. First Blood
6. The Raid

Nobody
7. Any Jason Statham movie

Boy kills world
8. Hard Boiled, Classic 80s cop/action film by John Woo starring Chow Yun Fat.
9. Shoot em' Up - Clive Owen 

## 4. Movies I should watch with Holly Hunter

**Score:** 36
**Body:** So I’m a huge fan of Holly Hunter’s filmography ever since I was introduced to her from The Incredibles & Incredibles 2 but I have a question for everyone are there any movies to watch with Holly Hunter where she isn’t voice acting?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Raising Arizona 
  - TURN TO THE RAUGHT!
2. The Piano. she won Best Actress for it
  - And she deserved it 100%.

And Anna Pacquin also deserved hers, for Best Supporting Actress, in her *first ever movie*... we really owe Jane Campion for discovering her.
3. Home for the Holidays 

O Brother Where Art Thou?

Copycat

Crash (1996) (Your mileage may vary with this one, no pun intended). 

Seconding recommendations for Raising Arizona and Broadcast News too...
4. Broadcast News is incredible. 
5. The Firm! Her role is minor, but it’s great.
6. lol. At first, I thought you were watching movies with her. Like the two of you and I was so excited. Anyway, love her broadcast news.
7. The Positively True Adventures of the Alleged Texas Cheerleader-Murdering Mom (1993)
8. Thirteen (2003)

## 5. Best 420 movies? 💨

**Score:** 29
**Body:** My wife and I are home with the day off and enjoying 420 💨 ✌️ 

We are currently settling in for a long overdue rewatch of Big Lebowski.

Need suggestions for the day to keep the vibe going…

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Friday 

How High 

Harold & Kumar Go to White Castle 
2. Grandma's Boy
3. Half Baked
4. Dazed and Confused. 

It’s like hanging out with your high school friends all over again. 
5. [Pineapple Express](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/10189-pineapple-express) (2008) and [This Is the End](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/109414-this-is-the-end) (2013) which contains a mini Pineapple Express 2
6. Super troopers 

Dude, where’s my car? -> I love this movie

Jay and Silent Bob strike back
7. Grandmas Boy, Dazed and Confused and Pineapple express 
8. As far as newer stuff goes i watched The Pizza Movie recently and rather enjoyed it. 
9. Tucker and Dale vs evil


They aren't stoners but it's a great watch while stoned 
10. Grandma's boy!  Greatest stoner movie ever written.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Whether she has an Oscar or not, Amy Adams is one of the best actresses of her generation.

**Score:** 2232

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|3oriOeYGl5MKFtb2FO)

Was snubbed for Arrival.
  - It’s a wonderful film and a very tough film to watch. I have to be in the right mood. I place it next to Interstellar as movies that touch the grief/loss/time/love buttons perfectly. 
    - My husband and I went to see Arrival in theaters not long after my grandmother, who was truly more like a mother to me, lost her battle with cancer. I thought I was walking into a sci-fi film, something distant and imaginative. A way to escape my sadness. But within the first few minutes, I was in tears.

Watching the quiet, tender moments of care on screen brought me right back to those months with her, the routines, the small acts of love, the way time seemed to both stretch and slip away. It felt so personal, so familiar, like my own story was unfolding in front of me. 

It was a sobering and very therapeutic experience for me.
    - Its the best movie I'll probably never watch again. It broke me emotionally lol. 
2. Sharp Objects was so good 
  - Sharp Objects haunted me, it was so so good. I have to say I was >!suspicious of the sister!< and the end was still a surprise.
3. An accomplished actress with some big movies, performances, highest paid actress at one time, acclaim and love from many audiences worldwide before someone calls her “underrated” or when is she going to get her flowers?

Definitely overlooked of an Oscar win but 6 nominations is incredible! One day she could easily win. 

I noticed she’s also got 2 Emmy nominations and 7 BAFTA nominations too. And won 2 Golden Globes out of 12 nominations. 

She’s doing fine. 
  - OP is a bot who farms karma with these condescending post titles
4. Oscars mean nothing.
5. The emotion when she said “ don’t you ever fart on my tits again” was powerful!

## 2. The incredibly talented Andy Serkis turns 62 today. Happy birthday to him!

**Score:** 1632

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Funny fact: Andy Serkis is a British actor of mixed heritage, born in Ruislip, London, to a British-born mother and an Iraqi-Armenian father. His father, Clement Serkis, was a doctor whose family name was originally Sarkissian. Serkis grew up in a Catholic household and spent time between London and Baghdad.
2. Tintin was so good. More people should have seen it.
3. My man got to star in two amazing trilogies!
4. Not to mention he did the audiobook for the trilogy as well. Incredible work if you haven't listened. 
5. Absolutely a generational talent. Every time I watch a movie with a CGI character, I guess that it's Serkis, and it turns out to be correct most of the time. 
6. It’s amazing how he can convey more emotion through a digital ape or Gollum than most actors can with their own faces.
7. The man is an artist!
8. I'm still waiting for an Oscar for him. But he'd probably need his own category. 
9. He deserves an Oscar.
10. Loved him in King Kong, The Batman, and Avengers: Age of Ultron!!

## 3. Just watched this criminally underrated gem and I can't express how much I love this movie now.

**Score:** 459

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’ll have to put this on my list, only heard good things!
  - If you want a 2 hour happy break from life, You've got to watch it
    - Love it - consider me sold!
2. The cast is AMAZING in this movie. From Brendan to this little girl, everyone gave a phenomenal performance. The cinematography, music, pacing, carrying multiple plots but never feeling like it's dragging or slowing down. Everything was perfect.
3. I thought it was really cute. I love a heartwarming movie.  While this wasn’t anything new or unpredictable it’s reliably a nice warm time.  
Fraser does a good job here but I was even more happy to see how well the supporting cast did around him too.  I am excited to see more from Hikari- only have exposure to her directing from this & a chunk of Beef, but that’s enough for me to be on board
4. I was very excited to watch this movie and would like to finish it, but as the plot started unfolding and explaining that his job would be to actively lie to a child, the whole thing just didn't sit right with me and I had a hard time going back to it. All the positive comments are making me think I should try again.
  - That's why the movie has that ending, Especially for the child part. You should definitely give this one a try 
5. Yes, very good movie. I think Brenden’s acting is actually better than The Whale where he won the Oscar for - also better writing. 
6. Should've been nominated and could've won.
7. Very beautifully brought together! 

Brendan just being the best and playing the role as faithfully as he could! 

## 4. Who are some of the most Evil movie characters that rarely get brought up in conversations?

**Score:** 447
**Body:**  Just rewatched *Juice*  a couple of days ago, have not watched it in years and it still hits way harder than I remembered.

Bishop to me is honestly one of the most unsettling movie characters in 90s cinema. What makes him so disturbing isn’t just that he becomes violent, it’s how *gradual* the shift is. At the start he feels like a typical insecure kid dealing with pressure and wanting respect, but you can literally watch that need for validation twist into full blown obsession with power and control.

By the time things really spiral, he’s not even reacting emotionally anymore. He’s just *deciding* people’s fates like it’s nothing. No hesitation, no real remorse, just cold calculation. And that’s what makes him scarier than a loud, chaotic villain. He’s calm, almost focused, like he’s finally found something that makes him feel important.

What really stuck with me is how he reframes betrayal and violence as “necessary” once he’s decided he’s in control. It’s not random anger it’s justification. He talks himself into being the only one who matters, and everyone else just becomes disposable.

Honestly, that slow burn descent from friend to full on antagonist is what makes Bishop so memorable to me. Tupac absolutely nailed it. Who are some other underrated movie villians?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Telly in KIDS. 
  - Agreed, despicable character, surprised he is rarely brought up
  - On that note Casper is a fucking scumbag pos too
2. Kevin Bacon in Sleepers 
  - Also Kevin Bacon in Hollow Man. 
3. Orin Scrivello, DDS. Played by Steve Martin in "Little Shop of horrors". Or even Audrey II who took over the world. *My girlfriend made me watch the 80s musical last night.
4. The Warden in Shawshank Redemption
  - ⬆️ 🎯

And the Bogs Diamond character who tormented Andy Dufresne in his early years there 
5. You got the juice now. 
6. Bishop in Juice has some characteristics of a horror villain. Such a great movie and cast 

## 5. What's the best modern historical epic movie?

**Score:** 439

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 300 was more of a comic than historical epic. 
2. Master and commander 
  - https://preview.redd.it/84nmbvh61dwg1.jpeg?width=4032&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=4b61636fbf05f6b0239bc9405336069f2b36c677

Simply one of the best movies, period. Swallowed up by LotR and Pirates of the Caribbean. Deserves so much more. There’s 20 freaking books and all amazing
3. 300 was historically the most accurate, Spartans definitely wore leather speedo's.
  - And fought literal monsters...
  - High fives for the women, and open mouth tongue kisses for the men. 
4. You missed Kingdom of Heaven (Directors Cut of course)
  - Was about to comment this. Kingdom of Heaven Director's Cut is like a different movie compared to the messy theatrical release. 
5. Last of the mohicans 
6. Gladiator. It’s genuinely a masterpiece

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The Fighter is not about boxing and I think that's why it still works fifteen years later

**Score:** 55
**Body:** Rewatched it last weekend for the first time since it came out and I was ready for it to feel dated the way a lot of those mid budget prestige dramas from that period do now, competent and well intentioned and kind of airless. It doesn't feel that way at all. The boxing is almost beside the point, it's window dressing for a film that's really about what a family does to a person who's trying to become something, how love and suffocation can be genuinely indistinguishable from each other when you're inside it, and how hard it is to leave a version of yourself behind when the people who built that version are still in the room. Micky Ward is trying to have a career and a life and the film understands that both of those things are impossible as long as he keeps choosing his family over himself and it never lets him off the hook for how long that takes.

What I forgot was how good Christian Bale actually is in it, not in the showy way the awards attention framed it at the time but in the specific uncomfortable way where you can see Dicky Eklund's intelligence and his delusion existing in the same person simultaneously and neither one cancels the other out. There's a scene where he watches the HBO documentary about himself in a room full of people who are watching him watch it and the thing Bale does with his face in that scene is the whole performance in one moment. 

The thing that stays with me after rewatching it is how unglamorous it is about everything, the poverty, the addiction, the small city dead end feeling of Lowell Massachusetts and it never uses any of that as texture or atmosphere the way a lot of films do, it just treats it as the actual conditions of these people's lives and lets that sit without comment. David O. Russell made a mess of a lot of things in that period but he got this one completely right and I don't think it gets enough credit for how well it has aged compared to basically everything else that was in its awards conversation that year.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’m gonna put some respect to Amy Adams’ and Melissa Leo’s performances as well.

The subtle voice crack and the near-tear thing that Amy Adams does when she’s asked what’s she done with her life and she defiantly (and perhaps sadly) reassures herself that she likes her life. Then continues to confess every regret. Such a vulnerable performance from a simple “tough as nails” type that this role would’ve otherwise been in a lesser actor imo

And Melissa Leo was a hoot and a half, a total scene stealer. She’s like the HBIC of this giant chorus in the form of the sisters like in a Greek play. The entire scene at Micky’s house is just gold standard passive aggressiveness + the “I’m sorry. I don’t know who you are and why you’re tawkin’” stays in my head rent free. 

As for the film, I think it’s super rewatchable and Russell isn’t like a “deep” director in terms of themes imo but gets really great character work out of a lot of his actors.
2. Very few sports movies are about the sport or the specific sporting event they’re depicting. That’s the domain of documentary filmmaking, not feature film making. Chariots of Fire isn’t a movie about a race, Friday Night Lights isn’t a movie about high school football. 

The Fighter and almost every other sports movie is just using the sport as the vehicle to talk about something else, usually human struggle. Struggle against the self, struggle against the natural environment, or other people, or circumstances blah blah blah.
  - I was just going to say the exact same thing. Not one film "about boxing" is really about boxing.
  - Someone once described Rocky as not a boxing movie but a gritty New Hollywood drama with 10 minutes of boxing at the end, and I think that's right on the money. 
    - Rocky to me is a misfit love story about 2 people who have no one else who loves them for who they are, find and love each other.  Btw, I love Rocky and gritty New Hollywood drama is spot on. 
3. Great film. So damn funny. You're right about Bale, he's so deep into his role here. Leo is nuts. I genuinely like David O. Russel's work, from Three Kings on. I hope he finds his stride again. He was really good at a certain type of movie. 
4. I’m a big fight fan and I’d say all the great combat sports films are ultimately really well done melodramas. The callousness of that world makes a perfect backdrop for these kinds of stories. And visually, fighting only really works as a metaphor. Very few convincing or compelling fight scenes in films. It’s the other stuff that works. 

## 2. In Taxi Driver (1976) is Travis Bickle's concern for Iris a redeeming factor/quality?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I view Travis as a tragic character. Circumstances (post war stress, poor status, lack of friends/family close to him, loneliness and the terrible conditions of the city at the time as shown) were such that he was socially unusual and purposeless, which led him to a dark/depressing path where he developed violent tendencies. A big driving factor towards this was the rejection by Betsy. His attitude towards Betsy later on, as well as violent/obsessed nature make some call him one of cinema's most well known "incel". Which of course has a negative connotation. Furthermore, a very active security agent spots him before he could commit a horrible crime and Travis would have been painted as THE villain and monster.

However, fortune favors him and he directs his energy elsewhere which causes him to be viewed as some kind of hero by the media. While Travis is a very complicated , unimpressive and delusional figure, his treatment of Iris is admirable in some ways. Clearly, he has no self serving motive to help Iris, other than to make himself feel better for doing something meaningful. But, he does not want anything in return from Iris. He frees her and even plans to pass on his savings to her. There seems to be a genuine concern for her well-being.

All this is to show that he is not just a monster, but a complex guy with shades of good and bad.

Does the ending event change Bickle? He acts normally with Betsy and seems to have moved on in the end. Was saving Iris and what followed gave him a relatively positive moral compass than before? From what I have read, Marty and Paul Schraeder (the writer) seem to think that he is the same as before.

However, my reading into the ending is that some human connection (with Iris) and getting some positive energy directed towards him by the city presumably, makes Bickle a somewhat changed person. Thoughts?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I always saw it as the idea that in Travis's head, assassinating the politician and killing the pimps/freeing Iris were equivalent. He saw both as some type of society rot that needed to be cleaned up. The irony is that one would get him treated like a horrifying villain and one would get him treated like a vigilante hero. And so whether the action was bad or good actually has nothing to do with the person committing it, and everything to do with the story that society constructs around it. 

It's impossible to tell whether he's really changed or not, but it also doesn't really matter because it's not about him.
  - I always thought that the insinuation was that he hadn't changed, but had been given license by the events of the film to keep searching for new prey.  The question isn't will he stop, but will his next victim be "acceptable" or not?
2. The original script had the pimps being Black but Scorsese was worried the movie would be taken the wrong way if that were the case. You can see hints of Travis' racism through his eyes throughout the movie. 


I'm not sure if the original Iris was also meant to be Black, but if she wasn't, then the "save Iris" storyline becomes very different. 
3. Remember when he points the gun at the TV when people are dancing to Jackson Brown's too late for the sky, he's pointing the gun at you. 

Travis's character is best summed up with the TV scene he keeps it on the edge of the milk crate and rocks it and eventually kicks it over breaking it. He experienced a negative emotion after that and it's something that he caused. He refuses to change and comes to the conclusion that he shouldn't despite it himself who caused all of his own misery. He believes he needs to save Iris but it's only his own self fulfilling prophecy.
4. No. Like The Searchers and Paris, Texas they are all trying to save women who do not want to be rescued or consider themselves in danger. 

This is the thing all the films and characters have in common and this is more than i planned to write on this. It does not make them redeemable, one could argue the opposite. 
  - While I agree with your overall sentiment, I do think that it's fair to point out that Iris WAS in a situation where she was in danger and needed "rescued" for lack of better term. 

That said Bickle's action wasn't selfless at all and was motivated mostly by his desire to see himself as some sort of avenging force and had little to do with Iris. 
  - I think this strongly applies to Betsy, but not really to Iris. She is a child who's being exploited and has no idea of how bad her situation is because she's literally 14. Looking at this you can even point out the distorted way Travis views women and people in general: to him, Betsy (a grown, responsible, functional woman working a supposedly high-paying job) and Iris (a literal child prostitute) are the exact same type of innocent creatures that need to be rescued from filth. 

In Travis head, killing the politician in order to "save" Betsy was not that much different from killing the gangsters in order to free Iris.
  - Ehhh its not redeemable but not because "she didnt want to be saved." Shes fucking 14! She didnt know any better and needed to be saved.


It's not redeemable because there's some attraction from Bickle's part, imo. He's only saving her out of self-interest, not because he believes it's the right thing to do.
  - The description of Iris, a trafficked 12 year old, as a “woman who doesn’t want to be rescued” is doing a AAAWWWFUL LOT of work.
  - “women who do not want to be rescued or consider themselves in danger.”

She’s a child prostitute, what are you talking about?

His motivations are selfish, but she is a child who is not equipped to make that decision.


## 3. Late Spring (1949 dir. Yasujirō Ozu)

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Does anyone get the sense that there's something opaque about the character of Noriko? Why exactly is she so resistant to getting married? She gives at least two reasons for not wanting to (she doesn't like the idea of arranged marriages, she's too happy living with her father), so is the viewer supposed to infer that there's another reason she's not talking about? Or is Ozu saying that *why* Noriko doesn't want to marry is less important than the basic fact of her resistance - in other words, that she's a symbol for the position of women caught between patriarchy and autonomy in postwar Japan?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don’t know that Ozu is deeply interested in analyzing people beyond the level of preference. Which is to say that “she had a preference” is sufficient for the character and it allows enough undefined space for viewers to fill in the blanks.

One thing Ozu films do that is special to me is leaving some things so clear in their real world application but unwritten as far as motivations, backstory, politics. Viewers abhor a vacuum, so to speak, and will fill in those empty spaces with their own emotions. Even if that doesn’t mean knowing a particular canonical backstory, every viewer of Late Spring “gets” who she is and empathizes with it to some degree.
2. When talking about Late Spring it is at least worth keeping in mind that Yasujirō Ozu was a man who spent his entire life without any known romantic relationships, never married, and lived with his more for his entire life until she passed away just two years before he did. As a result of this there has been a lot of people speculating on if Ozu was gay or asexual.  
 
I see Noriko in Late Spring as a character that can stand in for just about anybody being asked to conform to a societal standard that is antithetical to their being. Regardless of her reasons for not wanting to get married the fact is that she is happy with the her life and doesn't want it changed. Regardless of what her true reasons are for not wanting to get married I don't think it really matters. The nail that sticks out gets hammered down. 
 
Ozu's films often featured characters that push against social mores and they are often hammered  down as a result, though some do manage to resist. Their reasons for clashing with what is expected of them isn't really that important in my opinion. Why someone feels confined by what society expects of them (at least in the situations Ozu presents) is not nearly as important as how they are treated as a result.
3. You ask an interesting question. I honestly didn't think of Noriko acting as a stand-in for the positive changes in the treatment of women in that era. Rather I thought that her not wanting to get married is reason enough to make such a decision.

Ozu keeps the backstory open ended so I think he wants the viewers to fill in the details as they see fit. It can't be that he missed giving her one because several of his films bear resemblance to Late Spring and from which one's I have seen, none of them have a back story for Noriko.
  - Or if not a heavy-handed and allegorical representative of changing times, it might still be fair to say Ozu wants to look at modernization and Americanization that was occurring and question it. It seems good, maybe. Is it good? Should we dive in to the future at the expense of the past? 

Critics often call Ozu flatly conservative because of his emphasis on family values and prewar tradition. He certainly cares about those things as story ingredients. But to me it has always felt more like a passive “these situations will arise now, so let’s see how they might play out.” In the same way that, say, Shakespeare used marriage plots of wealth and status. 

When it comes to the actual 1940s/50s/60s Japan he isn’t truly neutral in moralizing but there are enough attempts at empathy from all sides of the argument — stubborn old fashioned fathers, progressive opportunistic young women, ambivalent young men— that it always seemed to me like his ultimate aim was enjoyment of the sport. The situations will unravel, will produce drama and meaning, and the overall tide of change isn’t something we can reverse but only sit and observe.
    - I agree with you. Earlier I too thought he would be on the conservative end completely but his works do carry signs of the changing times. His female characters have more agency too, compared to Naruse and Mizoguchi.

Rather than painting women as Priestesses, princesses or prostitutes he paints them as multi faceted individuals with fleshed out personalities. 

Compared to the 60s filmmakers he definitely appears older in beliefs but he was quite modern for his generation.
4. Ozu movies were never too deep. I loved those primarily because of the masterpiece compositions and cinematography. Story wise, all his movies were very simple and based on what was prominant in society those days. The idea of a women not wanting to get married was not common at that time in traditional asian society so it created a nice arch to show a women challenging a norm. 

## 4. Want to briefly talk about Mario Movie world logic ( Old and new) and storytelling in general

**Score:** 5
**Body:** People keep bringing up the logic and characters of the new Mario movies as a point of criticism.

  
The first film almost does absolutly nothing in terms of explaining itself and I think there’s a very specific reason why:

  
1994 Supermario Bros. Movie.

See to me The SMB 90s movie is an excercise in what happens when you take a simple premise and attempt to explain it fully.

  
With Mario you have a simple idea:  An Italian plumber runs through a fantasy world of mushrooms evil turtles power ups and a princess.

  
So when you make a movie the temptation arrives as “So wait - what is this world? Why is this plumber here? evil toadstools? A dinosaur? Power ups? How did all this come to be?”

  
And suddenly the answer starts to materialize as “Well- it kinda feel like maybe this is the future. Maybe this is actually a dystopian landscape with evolved dinosaurs. Maybe a dimension parralel To ours even. Maybe the power ups are some kind of high tech technology. Maybe the mushrooms are like a science experiment,

Thats it- this all makes sense when you realize this is a cyberpunk future scape and the plumber is a lost human protecting an evolved mushroom organism.” 

  
And then suddenly - boom you have The 90s Mario movie a film that finally told audiences what the hell the actual Mario world is- and it’s a nightmare.

  
Sometimes explaining things defeats the purpose of why they are there. In the Mario movie there are floating bricks, there are magic power ups and living plants. The new movie learned from the old film realized the answer has to be “Why? Just Becuase, that’s why.”

As much as I love the Mario franchise of games the thing that always drew me in was that they were taking familiar elements and always introducing something new. There was a sense of wonder at something weird and interesting around every corner. It felt completely counter to what I enjoy of those games to then make a movie that just pushes the same junk I already know at me.

And yeah, it gets defended as “just a kids movie,” “just a movie for the fans,” “it’s not trying to win any awards!!” As though there haven’t been countless movies that fit that criteria that are also creative, intelligent, inventive, and feature fantastic craftsmanship?
  - I think what's tough is that pleasing everyone in adapting a game like Mario that has virtually zero cohesive lore is pretty much an impossible task. Make it shallow and appealing to kids and older fans get mad that it's not creative enough. I guarantee if they got more creative with it though fans would be pissed that it's disrespectful to the source material because naturally being more creative would involve developing character motivations and lore explanations that just aren't in the games. 

Honestly I don't really mind that these films are basically just love letters to the series. My 5 year old loves watching the plumbers fight the turtles and save the princess, and I like looking for easter eggs, it's a win-win. I didn't need a super deep film adaptation about a very shallow series lorewise.

This is not something a movie based on a beloved IP can consider at all. 

With a game you spend 20+ hours with most likely the first time around. You get used and appreciate all the new things as they slowly come up. A movie just doesn't have that time to ease you into new things, and they would be much to jarring.

4. I don't have a problem with either one's approach, personally - where they falter is in scene to scene narrative dynamics, not how they adapt the source material. 

I find the 1993 Film to be a metric ton of fun, both for what's on screen and how it recontextualizes the early Mario games - because it's a hodgepodge of the first four mainline games (which it cribs a ton from), cyberpunk, The Princess Bride and a ton of other things - and for all its behind the scenes drama. It's imaginative, if messy! If it had completely pulled off what it was going for, everybody would be calling it a brilliant exercise in adaptation, but they didn't - which brings up another conversation about whether there are any bad ideas or simply bad executions. 

These new Films - the first one at least, haven't seen the second - take a more loyal approach that everybody called "too safe," but I think it's pretty interesting and isn't too dissimilar from the approach I've always wanted to take with a Sonic Film. That being: accept the world and the source material for exactly what it is, and don't make any excuses for it. If you want to explain something weird, take it for granted - don't recontextualize anything for real ism's sake. That's interesting *because* it's novel. 

I liked the first Film a lot, but where it really felt too safe was in it's pace and tone - the Mario games have always been for everybody, and if you look at earlier screenplays for the 1993 Film they really get this mix of tones down pat. The closest I can think of is like. . .*The Princess Bride.* These new Films hew a little too far to the younger side of the Family Film quadrant, and their humor is a little too obvious and easy, but then I also say the same thing about these Sonic movies and everyone loves them, so -
5. Yes, it's not really something that should have ever had a live-action version attempted; it works better as an animated kids thing, just like every animated kids thing ever made.

If you try to apply logic to...I don't know, why Bugs Bunny can talk...it would completely fall apart.

Even something like the Jim Carrey Grinch movie; people love that film, it adheres to its own internal logic, and yet to me it has an uncanny valley aspect to it, because the whole thing is based on goofy drawings/animation.

As far as the new films go, I don't have kids, so I don't really feel compelled to watch them. Even if I was, casting Chris Pratt as Mario was always such an odd choice to me, I don't think I could  really ever get beyond it.

## 5. De-tachment (2011) movie discussion?

**Score:** 1
**Body:** Detachment (2011) — just finished it, wanna hear what others think

I just finished watching Detachment (2011) and I sat through the whole thing without a single break. That's rare for me, I need something thought provoking or I can't sit still.

At first I really thought it was gonna be like every other teacher movie, yk. The special teacher who comes in, puts in the work, makes the unruly students lives better. At least until the school year ends ig. The kind of movie that wraps everything up nicely and leaves you feeling good about the world.

But it wasn't that. It was realistic. As realistic as a 2011 film could be. The acting, the dialogues, the cinematography, all of it was so good. Not a movie for kids though. There's this constant heaviness to it, like everyone in the film is just barely keeping it together. The students, the teachers, everyone. And it never really lets up.

Adrien Brody was incredible in this. The way he plays someone who clearly cares but is also so disconnected from everything around him, it felt very real. You can see it in his face more than his words.

The ending was something else to me. The protagonist just teaching in this messed up, almost abandoned classroom. I don't fully know what to make of it and that's exactly why I wanna hear from other people who watched it. What did you think of that ending? What did you take from the whole thing?

Would love to hear different perspectives on it.

(For context, the movie follows a substitute teacher sent to one of the lowest rated schools in the state. Is he there to teach, to help, to make things better? Maybe not.)
